en.extract:
The black hawk-eagle (Spizaetus tyrannus), also known as the tyrant hawk-eagle, is a species of eagle found from central Mexico through Central America into the south of Brazil to Colombia, eastern Peru, and as far as northern Argentina. There are two known subspecies, S.t. tyrannus, which is found in Southeastern Brazil and Northeastern Argentina, and the slightly smaller S. t. serus, which can be found elsewhere throughout the species' range. Its preferred habitats include humid and moist forests close to rivers, and several types of woodland. It is uncommon to fairly common throughout most of its range. Its closest relative is the ornate hawk-eagle, which is similar in size, appearance and behavior but lives at lower elevations.


Description

The black hawk-eagle is 58–70 cm (23–27.5 in) long and weighs about 900 to 1,300 grams (2.0 to 2.9 pounds). It has black plumage with varying patterns on its wings and body, and white speckling in places. It has barred wings, slightly elliptical in shape, and a long, narrow tail which is rarely fanned. The four grey bars on the tail are distinctive to the black hawk-eagle, as is the white line seen slightly above the bird's eye. While flying, the broadness and shortness of the wings become apparent. While in flight, the bird's tail is typically kept closed.


Diet
Though light and small compared to other eagles, this bird is a powerful predator that frequently hunts relatively large prey. It mainly eats large rodents (such as lowland pacas,squirrels), opossums and monkeys (such as howler monkeys, marmosets and squirrel monkeys), as well as, occasionally, bats, birds (even as small as the social flycatcher and passerine nestlings) and some reptiles (such as large lizards, including iguanas, and snakes). Its popular name in Brazil is "Gavião-pega-macaco", which means "hawk catching-monkey". The birds it takes can be quite large, such as toucans, macaws, guans and chachalacas.


Behavior
The black hawk-eagle is a solitary bird who only ever encounter each other during a territory feud or during mating season. When they hunt down prey they look briefly atop perches for anything and if they don't find anything they fly to another perch. Upon finding prey they are taken by pouncing or tail-chasing.


Breeding

The black hawk-eagle's breeding behaviour is little known. In a study carried out in Guatemala by The Peregrine Fund, four nests were documented. The average nest height was 25.5m and the nests were all built in lateral limbs away from the center of the tree. All known breeding pairs, both in the wild and in captivity, have laid single egg clutches and the estimating incubation period is 44 days. During one study it was found that Black hawk-eagles have been noted to lay more during heavy rainfall.

The '''black hawk-eagle''' ('''{{lang|la|Spizaetus tyrannus}}'''), also known as the '''tyrant hawk-eagle''',<ref name="peregrinefund.org">{{cite web |title=Black Hawk-eagle |url=https://www.peregrinefund.org/explore-raptors-species/eagles/black-hawk-eagle |publisher=The Peregrine Fund}}</ref> is a species of [[eagle]] found from central [[Mexico]] through Central America<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=2020-05-07|title=Black Hawk-Eagles in Panama {{!}} Whitehawk Birding Blog|url=https://www.whitehawkbirding.com/black-hawk-eagles-in-panama/ |access-date=2020-06-28|language=en-US}}</ref> into the south of [[Brazil]] to Colombia, eastern [[Peru]], and as far as northern [[Argentina]].<ref name="first">{{cite web |url=http://www.hawk-conservancy.org/priors/blckheag.shtml |title=Black Hawk-Eagle – Spizaetus tyrannus|access-date=14 August 2007}}</ref> There are two known subspecies, {{lang|la|S.t. tyrannus}}, which is found in Southeastern Brazil and Northeastern Argentina, and the slightly smaller {{lang|la|S. t. serus}}, which can be found elsewhere throughout the species' range.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last1=Jenny |first1=J. Peter |last2=Whitacre |first2=David F. |year=2013 |title=Neotropical Birds of Prey: Biology and Ecology of a Forest Raptor Community |chapter=Black Hawk-eagle |publisher=Cornell University Press |via=Project MUSE|chapter-url=https://muse.jhu.edu/chapter/1706455 |pages=185–202}}</ref> Its preferred habitats include humid and moist forests close to rivers, and several types of woodland.<ref name="second">{{cite web |url=http://www.arthurgrosset.com/sabirds/blackhawk-eagle.html|title=Black Hawk-Eagle |access-date=14 August 2007}}</ref> It is uncommon to fairly common throughout most of its range. Its closest relative is the [[ornate hawk-eagle]], which is similar in size, appearance and behavior but lives at lower elevations.

==Description==
[[File:Black Hawk-Eagle.tif|left|thumb| [[Ailigandí]] area, [[Panama]]]]
[[File:Black Hawk-Eagle.jpg|thumb|left|A captive adult black hawk-eagle.]]
The black hawk-eagle is {{convert|58|–|70|cm|in|round=0.5|abbr=on}} long and weighs about {{convert|900 to 1,300|g|lb|abbr=off}}. It has black plumage with varying patterns on its wings and body, and white speckling in places. It has barred wings, slightly elliptical in shape, and a long, narrow tail which is rarely fanned. The four grey bars on the tail are distinctive to the black hawk-eagle, as is the white line seen slightly above the bird's eye. While flying, the broadness and shortness of the wings become apparent.<ref>''Birds of Venezuela'' by Steve Hilty. Princeton University Press, 2003</ref> While in flight, the bird's tail is typically kept closed.<ref name="first" /><ref name="second" />

==Diet==
Though light and small compared to other eagles, this bird is a powerful predator that frequently hunts relatively large prey. It mainly eats large rodents (such as [[lowland paca]]s,[[squirrel]]s), opossums and monkeys (such as [[howler monkey]]s, [[marmoset]]s and [[squirrel monkey]]s), as well as, occasionally, bats, birds (even as small as the [[social flycatcher]] and [[passerine]] nestlings) and some reptiles (such as large [[lizard]]s, including [[iguana]]s, and [[snake]]s).<ref name="peregrinefund.org"/><ref name=":0" /><ref>{{cite web |author=Evelyn Noyan |date=2017 |title=''Spizaetus tyrannus'' (Black Hawk-eagle) |work=The Online Guide to the Animals of Trinidad and Tobago |url=https://sta.uwi.edu/fst/lifesciences/sites/default/files/lifesciences/documents/ogatt/Spizaetus_tyrannus%20-%20Black%20Hawk-eagle.pdf |access-date=2023-12-13}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.globalraptors.org/grin/SpeciesResults.asp?specID=8078 |access-date=2020-06-28 |title=Spizaetus tyrannus |website=www.globalraptors.org |archive-date=2021-04-19  |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210419050259/http://globalraptors.org/grin/SpeciesResults.asp?specID=8078 |url-status=dead }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Ramírez Fernández |first1=José D. |last2=Biamonte |first2=Esteban |last3=Gutiérrez-Vannucchi |first3=Ana-Cecilia |last4=Sarria-Miller |first4=Gustavo A. |last5=Scott |first5=Alfredo |last6=Sandoval |first6=Luis |date=January 2019 |title=Previously undescribed food resources of eleven Neotropical bird species |journal=Boletín SAO |volume=28 |issue=1 & 2 |pages=1–8 |url=https://kerwa.ucr.ac.cr/bitstream/handle/10669/88524/Ramirez-Fernandez%20et%20al%202019.pdf?sequence=1&isAllowed=y |access-date=2023-12-11}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |vauthors=Ferguson-Lees J, Christie DA, Franklin K, Mead D, Burton P |year=2001 |title=Raptors of the world |series=Helm Identification Guides}}</ref> Its popular name in Brazil is "Gavião-pega-macaco", which means "hawk catching-monkey". The birds it takes can be quite large, such as [[toucan]]s, [[macaw]]s, [[Guan (bird)|guan]]s and [[chachalaca]]s.<ref name="peregrinefund.org"/><ref name="second" />

== Behavior ==
The black hawk-eagle is a solitary bird who only ever encounter each other during a territory feud or during mating season. When they hunt down prey they look briefly atop perches for anything and if they don't find anything they fly to another perch. Upon finding prey they are taken by pouncing or tail-chasing.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Black hawk-eagle |url=https://dwazoo.com/animal/black-hawk-eagle/ |access-date=2023-12-13 |website=The Dallas World Aquarium |language=en-US}}</ref>

==Breeding==
[[File:Black Hawk-eagle (40992649042).jpg|thumb|Wild juvenile black hawk-eagle.]]
The black hawk-eagle's breeding behaviour is little known. In a study carried out in Guatemala by [[The Peregrine Fund]], four nests were documented. The average nest height was 25.5m and the nests were all built in lateral limbs away from the center of the tree. All known breeding pairs, both in the wild and in captivity, have laid single egg clutches and the estimating incubation period is 44 days.<ref name=":1" /> During one study it was found that Black hawk-eagles have been noted to lay more during heavy rainfall.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Blank |first1=Marcel Henrique |last2=Guimarães Jr |first2=Paulo Roberto |last3=do Nascimento |first3=Lucas Ferreira |last4=Pereira |first4=Ricardo Jose Garcia |date=2023-03-16 |title=Ecological drivers of breeding periodicity in four forest neotropical eagles |journal=Scientific Reports |language=en |volume=13 |issue=1 |page=4385 |doi=10.1038/s41598-023-31274-8 |issn=2045-2322|doi-access=free |pmid=36928378 |pmc=10020578 |bibcode=2023NatSR..13.4385B }}</ref>
